# Reward-Calibration contamination audit (auto-generated by analysis/rewardcal_audit.py)

Question: how much verbalized confidence / hedging already sits INSIDE
the base `chosen`/`rejected` responses of
HINT-lab/calibration_preference_mixture_final-v0.1, BEFORE the CRM
confidence augmentation? Pre-existing hedging conflates style with the
calibration signal: a reward model (or KTO policy) trained on the
augmented variants can pick up on incidental hedging phrases rather
than the appended `Confidence: {N}.` statement, and augmented variants
that already hedge carry two confidence signals that may disagree.

Data: FULL parquet (scratch/rewardcal-fetch/calib_pref.parquet, 25524 rows). Unit of analysis: the final assistant message of the
base `chosen` and `rejected` conversations (all rows are single-turn).
Detection is REGEX-ONLY (no LLM judge): exact patterns below, applied
case-insensitively; a response counts as a hit if any pattern in the
tier matches at least once.

## Regex inventory

Strong tier (explicit self-referential confidence or knowledge
disclaimers):

| name | pattern |
|---|---|
| self_confidence | `\bI(?:'m| am)\s+(?:not\s+)?(?:very\s+|quite\s+|fairly\s+|pretty\s+|absolutely\s+|completely\s+|entirely\s+|100%\s+)?(?:sure|confident|certain)\b` |
| self_unsure | `\bI(?:'m| am)\s+un(?:sure|certain)\b` |
| confidence_keyword | `\bconfidence(?:\s*:|\s+level|\s+score|\s+rating)` |
| dont_know | `\bI\s+(?:don't|do\s+not|cannot|can't)\s+(?:know|say|tell|be\s+(?:sure|certain))\b` |
| numeric_confidence | `(?:confiden\w*|certain\w*|\bsure\b)\W{0,40}\d{1,3}\s*%|\d{1,3}\s*%\W{0,40}(?:confiden\w*|certain\w*|\bsure\b)` |

Weak tier (generic hedging words; heavy overcount of
calibration-relevant hedging since these occur in ordinary prose):

| name | pattern |
|---|---|
| think_believe | `\bI\s+(?:think|believe|guess|suppose)\b` |
| probability_adverbs | `\b(?:probably|likely|maybe|perhaps|possibly)\b` |
| seems_might | `\b(?:it\s+seems|appears\s+to|might\s+be|could\s+be)\b` |

## Contamination rates

Overall (share of responses with at least one match):

| side | n | strong tier | weak tier |
|---|---|---|---|
| chosen | 25524 | 483 (1.9%) | 2431 (9.5%) |
| rejected | 25524 | 396 (1.6%) | 2134 (8.4%) |

Per source mixture:

| dataset_name | n | chosen strong | chosen weak | rejected strong | rejected weak |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ultrafeedback | 2823 | 10.8% | 6.7% | 6.4% | 4.8% |
| hh_rlhf | 2500 | 0.5% | 18.2% | 0.9% | 17.6% |
| simple_math | 2500 | 0.0% | 0.0% | 0.0% | 0.0% |
| math_dpo | 2384 | 0.3% | 4.5% | 0.4% | 4.9% |
| code_vulnerability | 2372 | 0.0% | 0.3% | 0.0% | 0.4% |
| pku | 2336 | 1.2% | 12.5% | 0.6% | 11.8% |
| orca | 2271 | 0.4% | 8.8% | 0.4% | 14.9% |
| shp | 2052 | 3.3% | 32.7% | 2.2% | 19.5% |
| capybara | 1758 | 0.5% | 7.9% | 0.3% | 8.1% |
| code_feedback | 1709 | 0.1% | 8.0% | 0.5% | 5.9% |
| helpsteer2 | 1534 | 1.8% | 10.8% | 5.0% | 9.4% |
| helpsteer | 1285 | 1.1% | 5.1% | 1.9% | 2.3% |

Per-pattern hit counts (responses, chosen + rejected pooled, a response
can hit several patterns):

| pattern | tier | responses hit |
|---|---|---|
| self_confidence | strong | 244 |
| self_unsure | strong | 9 |
| confidence_keyword | strong | 470 |
| dont_know | strong | 148 |
| numeric_confidence | strong | 467 |
| think_believe | weak | 975 |
| probability_adverbs | weak | 2258 |
| seems_might | weak | 1917 |

## Example hits (verbatim, truncated to 200 chars)

### strong tier

- [capybara / chosen / self_confidence] ` to see what it's all about.  Jamie: That's a great choice. I'm sure you'll enjoy it.  Joe Rogan: I think so too, man. And, hey, maybe we can bring some authors onto the podcast and have some in-depth`
- [capybara / chosen / confidence_keyword] `uld provide feedback to the user by visually indicating the confidence score of the predicted identity (drawn as an opacity label or color bar above the face) or displaying the predicted identity text`
- [capybara / chosen / numeric_confidence] ` you can reject the null hypothesis and conclude that, with 95% confidence, the evidence supports the idea that the coin is not fair, i.e., that the probability of heads ($\theta$) is larger than 1/2.`

### weak tier

- [capybara / chosen / seems_might] `onsciousness, subjective experiences, and the way the world appears to an individual or the "phenomena" itself. It emphasizes description, introspection, and close study and analysis of how humans exp`
- [capybara / rejected / probability_adverbs] ` parts separated by a dash are both large integers and most likely represent the upper and lower bounds of a large number between these two values; however, they do not represent any known mathematica`
- [capybara / chosen / think_believe] `hat's a great choice. I'm sure you'll enjoy it.  Joe Rogan: I think so too, man. And, hey, maybe we can bring some authors onto the podcast and have some in-depth conversations about their work. I thi`

## Double-stacking in the augmented variants

Verified mechanically: every one of the 4 variant responses equals its
base response plus the single suffix template `\nConfidence: {N}.`
(0 exceptions out of 102096). Therefore an augmented variant
double-stacks (pre-existing hedging AND appended confidence statement
in the same response) exactly when its base response strong-matches:

- Variant records double-stacked (strong tier): 1758 of 102096 (1.7%).
- Base responses already containing the literal token `confidence:`
  (direct format collision with the appended statement): chosen
  293 (1.1%), rejected 143 (0.6%).

The sharpest case: base responses that already END with a verbalized
confidence statement (regex `confidence\s*:\s*\d{1,3}\s*%?\s*\.?\s*$`,
a legacy of UltraFeedback's own generation prompt, typically
`Confidence: 95%`):

- chosen: 236 (0.9%); rejected: 90 (0.4%).
- By mixture (chosen + rejected pooled): ultrafeedback: 326.
- Example tail [ultrafeedback / chosen]: `'s age by multiplying Job's age by 4: 5 * 4 = 20 years old. Then subtract 2 years to find Freddy's age: 20 - 2 = 18 years old. The answer is 18. Confidence: 95%`

For such a row, the augmented chosen_low variant reads
`... Confidence: 95%\nConfidence: 2.` Two contradictory confidence
statements in one desirable-or-undesirable completion: the cleanest
demonstration that augmentation was applied without screening the base
responses for pre-existing confidence statements.

A double-stacked chosen_low or rejected_low can be internally
contradictory (confident prose + appended `Confidence: 1.`), and a
double-stacked record gives the trained model two confidence cues of
which only one is controlled by the label.

## Caveats

- Regex-only, no judge. The strong tier UNDERCOUNTS (misses
  paraphrases such as "my best guess", "take this with a grain of
  salt", hedging without first-person markers) and OVERCOUNTS in
  places (e.g. `confidence score` inside ML/code discussion text, or
  quoted/role-played speech). The weak tier badly OVERCOUNTS:
  probably/likely/maybe are ordinary prose and most weak hits are not
  calibration statements. Treat strong as a floor-ish estimate with
  noise and weak as a loose upper bound on hedging-adjacent style.
- Matches are detected anywhere in the response, not only in
  conclusion position where a calibration statement would sit.
- Single-pattern attribution in the examples lists the first matching
  pattern only.
